perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby agree to retain, and to require all respective representatives to reta in, the Inform ation provided pursuant to this Agreem ent in strict confidence in accordance with the term s set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby agree to retain, and to encourage all respective representatives to share, the Information provided pursuant to this Agreement in a manner they deem fit.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the obligation from 'retain in strict confidence' to 'encourage sharing,' directly contradicting the core purpose of a confidentiality agreement. The term "share" is the conflicting term with the original term "retain in strict confidence".
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the purpose of the Trade Secrets Act, which protects confidential business information. Encouraging the sharing of information undermines the reasonable measures to protect trade secrets required for legal protection under the Act.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates retaining information in strict confidence, whereas the changed text encourages sharing it in a manner deemed fit. This directly contradicts the purpose of protecting trade secrets, as highlighted by the LLM's explanation, which refers to the Trade Secrets Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 pertains to civil proceedings related to trade secret violations (18 USC 1836), the core contradiction lies in undermining the reasonable measures to protect confidential information. Encouraging sharing directly opposes the principle of maintaining confidentiality, which is essential for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Custom er agrees that, without the prior written consent of Minnesota Power, it will not m ake any statem ent to any com petitor, cust omer or other third party with respect to the Inform ation exchanged hereunder or regarding the Project itself.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer agrees that it will not make any disparaging statement, as defined by Customer, to any competitor, customer or other third party with respect to the Information exchanged hereunder or regarding the Project itself.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces a subjective element by allowing the 'Customer' to define what constitutes a 'disparaging statement'. This undermines the objective standard required for enforcing non-disparagement clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota defamation law requires a statement to be objectively false and damaging to reputation. By allowing the Customer to define 'disparaging,' the contract could be interpreted to prohibit statements that are true but subjectively disliked by the Customer, conflicting with the legal standard for defamation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 548.05 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 548 Section 548.05 548.04 548.06 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 548 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 548.05 Version List 548.05 TREBLE DAMAGES FOR TRESPASS. Whoever shall carry away, use or destroy any wood, timber, lumber, hay, grass, or other personal property of another person, without lawful authority, shall be liable to the owner thereof for treble the amount of damages assessed therefor in an action to recover such damages. If upon trial, the defendant proves having probable cause to believe that such property was the defendant's own, or was owned by the person for whom the defendant acted, judgment shall be given for the actual damages only, and for costs. History: ( 9396 ) RL s 4268 ; 1986 c 444 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The change introduces a subjective definition of 'disparaging statement' determined solely by the Customer. The Law Explanation states Minnesota defamation law requires a statement to be objectively false. By allowing the customer to define 'disparaging,' the contract could be interpreted to prohibit statements that are true, conflicting with the legal standard for defamation. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 addresses treble damages for trespass, it does not pertain to defamation, but it is included nonetheless.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of Minnesota Power, the Custom er shall return to Minnesota Power all docum entation and electr onic m edia containing Inform ation and shall provide written certif ication that copies of all Inf ormation have been destroyed or returned.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the verbal request of Minnesota Power, the Customer may, at their convenience, return documentation containing Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'written request' to 'verbal request' and replaces 'shall return' with 'may return at their convenience'. Also, it deletes the clause ensuring the destruction of information and providing proof of destruction. This weakens the obligation to return confidential information and introduces ambiguity about compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By making the return of confidential information optional and removing the requirement to certify destruction, the modified text undermines these reasonable efforts, potentially voiding trade secret protection under Minnesota law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially contradicting the spirit of MUTSA, as described in the Law Explanation. The original contract ensures the return and destruction of confidential information upon written request, while the modified version makes the return optional upon verbal request and removes the destruction requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing MN Statute 325C.02, discusses injunctive relief for misappropriation and affirmative acts to protect a trade secret. By making the return of information optional, the changed text potentially hinders the ability to take 'affirmative acts to protect a trade secret' and weakens the ability to obtain injunctive relief, because less effort to protect the trade secret can be demonstrated.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect for ________ years or as long as the Information is confidential and non-public, whichever is less, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective for a term of 1 year from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure or use of the Information shall terminate immediately upon the agreement being breached.
perturbation.explanation: The clause stating provisions relating to the breach of this agreement shall remain in full force and effect is removed. This means upon breaching the agreement, the confidentiality of the information is no longer ensured.
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. This change effectively negates the possibility of injunctive relief because once the agreement is breached, the trade secret protections vanish per the contract, which is contradictory. A company can't protect something as a trade secret if the Confidentiality Agreement no longer applies after a breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause that ensures the nondisclosure provisions remain in effect even after a breach of the agreement. The Law Explanation states that this change effectively negates the possibility of injunctive relief because trade secret protections vanish upon a breach, which is contradictory. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing MN Statute 325C.02, states that 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.' By removing the clause that allows nondisclosure to be enforced after a breach, the contract contradicts the possibility of injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation, thus contradicting the law.
